## The Matrix: Worked Example

Let `T = 2`, `d = 4`, $d_{\text{ff}} = 8$.

Input (after attention):

```text
X = [[ 0.5,  1.2, -0.3,  0.8],
     [-0.1,  0.7,  0.9, -0.4]]    (2×4)
```

Let's trace just row 0 of X: `x = [0.5, 1.2, -0.3, 0.8]`

*Step 1 — Expand:* $h = x W_1$

```text
h = [1.1, -0.4, 0.8, 2.1, -0.3, 0.6, 1.5, -0.8]
```

*Step 2 — GELU:*

```text
GELU(1.1)  ≈ 0.95
GELU(-0.4) ≈ -0.12
GELU(0.8)  ≈ 0.64
GELU(2.1)  ≈ 2.06
GELU(-0.3) ≈ -0.11
GELU(0.6)  ≈ 0.44
GELU(1.5)  ≈ 1.40
GELU(-0.8) ≈ -0.17

h' = [0.95, -0.12, 0.64, 2.06, -0.11, 0.44, 1.40, -0.17]
```

*Step 3 — Contract:* $y = h' W_2$

```text
y ≈ [0.7, 1.1, -0.2, 0.9]
```

The output `y` is a transformed version of the input vector — same dimensionality, different values.
The transformation was learned to improve next-token prediction.

Figure @fig-ch08-matrix-ffn shows the feed-forward network expanding the hidden dimension, applying GELU, and contracting back to model width.

![Feed-forward network](images/ch06_ffn.png){#fig-ch08-matrix-ffn fig-alt="Feed-forward network — expand → GELU → contract."}

Figure @fig-ch08-matrix-memory shows the FFN interpretation as associative memory, with keys and values stored in its weight matrices.

![Feed-forward network as associative memory](images/ch06_memory.png){#fig-ch08-matrix-memory fig-alt="The FFN as associative memory — keys and values stored in the weight matrices."}
